quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of force?

options.text: Joule (J)
Pascal (Pa)
Watt (W)
Newton (N)

quiz_questions.explanation: SI units: Force = Newton (N = kg·m/s²), Energy/Work = Joule (J = kg·m²/s²), Power = Watt (W = J/s), Momentum = kg·m/s. 1 horsepower ≈ 746 W. The answer is 'Newton (N)'. The other options — 'Joule (J)', 'Watt (W)', and 'Pascal (Pa)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a free-body diagram?

options.text: A graph of force vs. time
A diagram showing all forces on a single object as arrows
A diagram of an object in free fall
A picture of an object with no forces

quiz_questions.explanation: Free-Body Diagrams A free-body diagram shows all forces acting on a single object as arrows from its center of mass. The other options — 'A diagram of an object in free fall', 'A graph of force vs. time', and 'A picture of an object with no forces' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the net force?

options.text: Any single force on the object
The force of gravity
The largest force acting on an object
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object

quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is 'The vector sum of all forces acting on an object'. The other options — 'The largest force acting on an object', 'The force of gravity', and 'Any single force on the object' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens when the net force on an object is zero?

options.text: The object must be at rest
The object must be moving
The object accelerates
The object is in equilibrium — no acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is 'The object is in equilibrium — no acceleration'. The other options — 'The object must be at rest', 'The object must be moving', and 'The object accelerates' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is force a scalar or vector?

options.text: Vector
Scalar
Neither
Both

quiz_questions.explanation: Vectors have both magnitude and direction (velocity, force, acceleration). Scalars have only magnitude (speed, mass, energy). Vectors can be decomposed into perpendicular components. The answer is 'Vector'. The other options — 'Scalar', 'Both', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a contact force?

options.text: A force acting at a distance
A force from gravity
An electromagnetic force
A force requiring physical touch

quiz_questions.explanation: Types of Forces Contact forces : Require physical touch — friction, tension, normal force, applied force, air resistance. The other options — 'A force acting at a distance', 'A force from gravity', and 'An electromagnetic force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 1 Newton in base units?

options.text: 1 kg·m/s
1 kg·m²/s²
1 kg/m·s²
1 kg·m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: Derived units are combinations of base units. Newton (N) = kg·m/s² (force). Joule (J) = kg·m²/s² (energy). Watt (W) = J/s (power). Pascal (Pa) = N/m² (pressure). The answer is '1 kg·m/s²'. The other options — '1 kg·m/s', '1 kg·m²/s²', and '1 kg/m·s²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is an example of a non-contact force?

options.text: Friction between shoes and the floor
Gravity pulling an apple downward
Tension in a stretched rubber band
Normal force from a table surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Non-contact forces act at a distance without physical touch. Gravity is the classic example — it pulls objects toward each other without requiring contact. Friction, tension, and normal force all require surfaces or objects to be in direct contact.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the arrows in a free-body diagram used to represent?

options.text: The speed of the object in each direction
The temperature changes of the object
The magnitude and direction of each force
The distance the object has traveled

quiz_questions.explanation: In a free-body diagram, each arrow represents a force. The length of the arrow shows the magnitude (strength) of the force, and the direction of the arrow shows which way the force acts. Longer arrows mean stronger forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many forces can act on a single object at the same time?

options.text: Only one force at a time
Exactly two forces at a time
Multiple forces can act simultaneously
No more than four forces at once

quiz_questions.explanation: Many forces can act on a single object at the same time. For example, a book on a table has gravity pulling it down and the normal force pushing it up. A sliding box may also experience friction and an applied push. The net force is the vector sum of all these forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of a force in physics?

options.text: A push or pull that can change an object's motion
The total energy stored inside an object
The rate at which an object covers distance
The amount of matter contained in an object

quiz_questions.explanation: A force is defined as a push or pull that acts on an object. Forces can cause an object to start moving, stop moving, speed up, slow down, or change direction. Force is measured in Newtons (N).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'net force' mean?

options.text: The strongest individual force on an object
The combined effect of all forces acting on an object
The force that always points downward on objects
A force applied only by a person or machine

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force is the overall force on an object after all individual forces are combined (added as vectors). When forces act in the same direction they add together; when they act in opposite directions they partially cancel. If the net force is zero, the object does not accelerate.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define the term 'equilibrium' as it applies to forces.

options.text: A condition where all forces are as large as possible
A condition where the net force on an object is zero
A condition where only gravity acts on the object
A condition where all motion has completely stopped

quiz_questions.explanation: Equilibrium means the net force acting on an object is zero (ΣF = 0). This does not require the object to be at rest — it can be moving at constant velocity. The key requirement is that all forces balance out so there is no acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of the term 'vector' in physics?

options.text: A quantity that has only magnitude and no direction
A quantity that has both magnitude and direction
A unit of measurement used for temperature
A type of graph used to display force data

quiz_questions.explanation: A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude (size or amount) and direction. Force, velocity, and acceleration are all vectors. By contrast, scalars such as mass, speed, and temperature have magnitude only.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'applied force' refer to?

options.text: The gravitational pull from Earth on an object
A push or pull exerted on an object by a person or another object
The upward force a surface exerts on an object
The resistance an object encounters from the air

quiz_questions.explanation: An applied force is a push or pull that is directly exerted on an object by a person, machine, or another object in contact with it. Examples include pushing a cart or pulling a wagon. It is a contact force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'gravity' as a force?

options.text: A contact force that requires objects to touch each other
A force that only acts on very heavy objects in space
An attractive force between any two objects that have mass
A repulsive force that pushes objects apart from each other

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravity is a non-contact, attractive force that exists between any two objects with mass. On Earth's surface, gravitational acceleration is approximately 9.8 m/s². The weight of an object equals its mass times gravitational acceleration (W = mg).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'balanced forces' mean?

options.text: Forces that cause an object to accelerate quickly
Forces that are equal in size but act in the same direction
Forces that cancel out so the net force is zero
Forces that only act on objects that are not moving

quiz_questions.explanation: Balanced forces are forces that are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, resulting in a net force of zero. When forces are balanced, the object's motion does not change — it stays at rest or continues moving at constant velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'unbalanced forces' describe?

options.text: Forces that perfectly cancel each other out
Forces that result in a nonzero net force and cause acceleration
Forces that are too small to have any measurable effect
Forces that only exist in the absence of gravity

quiz_questions.explanation: Unbalanced forces do not cancel out, producing a nonzero net force. According to Newton's Second Law, a nonzero net force causes an object to accelerate — it speeds up, slows down, or changes direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'air resistance'?

options.text: A force that pushes objects upward against gravity
A contact force that opposes an object's motion through air
A non-contact force that attracts nearby air particles
A force created only when the wind is blowing outside

quiz_questions.explanation: Air resistance (also called drag) is a contact force that opposes the motion of an object as it moves through air. It depends on the object's speed, shape, and surface area. Faster objects and larger surface areas experience more air resistance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'magnitude' mean when describing a force?

options.text: The direction in which the force is applied
The size or strength of the force in Newtons
The type of force such as gravity or friction
The object on which the force is being exerted

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnitude refers to the size or strength of a force, expressed as a number with units (Newtons). For example, a 10 N force has a magnitude of 10 N. Since force is a vector, you need both magnitude and direction to fully describe it.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a book on a table. The book does not move. What can the student conclude about the forces on the book?

options.text: No forces are acting on the book at all
Only gravity is acting on the book right now
The forces on the book are balanced so the net force is zero
The table is pushing the book upward harder than gravity pulls it down

quiz_questions.explanation: A stationary book on a table has two main forces: gravity pulling it down and the normal force pushing it up. Since the book is not accelerating, these forces are balanced and the net force is zero. Forces still act on the book — they simply cancel out.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, two students push a cart in opposite directions. Student A pushes with 20 N to the right, and Student B pushes with 15 N to the left. What is the net force on the cart?

options.text: 35 N to the right
5 N to the right
5 N to the left
35 N to the left

quiz_questions.explanation: When forces act in opposite directions, the net force is the difference between them and points in the direction of the larger force. Here: 20 N (right) − 15 N (left) = 5 N to the right. The cart accelerates to the right.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student draws a free-body diagram for a falling skydiver. Which forces should appear on the diagram?

options.text: Only the force of gravity pointing downward
Gravity pointing downward and air resistance pointing upward
Normal force pointing upward and gravity pointing downward
Thrust pointing downward and friction pointing sideways

quiz_questions.explanation: A falling skydiver has two forces: gravity pulling downward and air resistance (drag) pushing upward against the direction of motion. There is no normal force because the skydiver is not touching a surface, and there is no thrust.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student pushes a box across the floor with a steady 30 N force. The box moves at a constant speed. What is the friction force on the box?

options.text: 0 N because the box is already moving
30 N opposing the direction of motion
60 N opposing the direction of motion
15 N opposing the direction of motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant speed means zero acceleration, which means the net force is zero. If the student pushes with 30 N forward, friction must be exactly 30 N backward to balance it out. The forces are balanced even though the box is moving.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a tug-of-war, Team A pulls with 500 N to the left and Team B pulls with 450 N to the right. Which way does the rope move?

options.text: The rope does not move because the forces cancel
The rope moves to the left toward Team A
The rope moves to the right toward Team B
The rope moves upward away from both teams

quiz_questions.explanation: The net force is 500 N − 450 N = 50 N to the left. Since the forces are unbalanced, the rope accelerates in the direction of the larger force — toward Team A on the left.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student holds a ball in her hand without moving. She then opens her hand and the ball falls. What force caused the ball to start moving?

options.text: An applied force from her hand pushing the ball down
The unbalanced gravitational force once the normal force was removed
Air resistance pushing the ball toward the ground below
Friction between the ball and the surrounding air molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: While the student held the ball, gravity and the normal force from her hand were balanced. When she opened her hand, the normal force disappeared, leaving gravity as an unbalanced force. The ball then accelerated downward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A soccer player kicks a ball on a grass field. The ball gradually slows down and stops. Which force is mainly responsible for stopping the ball?

options.text: The gravitational pull of the Earth on the ball
Friction between the ball and the grass surface
The normal force from the ground pushing upward
The tension in the grass blades pulling on the ball

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction is a contact force that opposes the relative motion between two surfaces. As the ball rolls on the grass, friction acts opposite to its direction of motion, gradually slowing it down until it stops.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that a magnet attracts a paper clip from across a table without touching it. What type of force is the magnet exerting?

options.text: A contact force because the magnet moves the clip
A non-contact force because it acts over a distance
A frictional force between the clip and the table
A gravitational force between the clip and the magnet

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnetic force is a non-contact force because the magnet can attract or repel objects without physically touching them. Non-contact forces — including gravity, magnetic force, and electric force — can act across a distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab experiment, a student measures two forces acting on a toy car: 8 N to the east and 8 N to the west. What happens to the car?

options.text: The car accelerates to the east due to the first force
The car accelerates to the west due to the second force
The car does not accelerate because the net force is zero
The car spins in a circle from the two equal forces

quiz_questions.explanation: When two equal forces act in opposite directions, they cancel each other out. The net force is 8 N − 8 N = 0 N. With zero net force, the car does not accelerate — it either stays at rest or continues at constant velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is riding a bicycle and suddenly applies the brakes. The bike slows down. What is the main contact force responsible for slowing the bicycle?

options.text: Gravity pulling the bicycle toward the ground below
The normal force from the road pushing the tires upward
The magnetic force between the brake pads and the wheel
Friction between the brake pads and the wheel rim

quiz_questions.explanation: When the student squeezes the brakes, the brake pads press against the wheel rim, creating friction. This friction force opposes the wheel's rotation and slows the bicycle down. Friction is a contact force that requires physical touch between surfaces.
